title: Tapping for Investing Isn't for Me
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the belief that investment decisions belong to a different kind of person
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the feeling that investing is too complicated, too risky, or simply not something women like you do. Taps through the exclusion story and toward the capacity to look at investing as a practical question rather than a class or gender boundary.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when investing feels like something that belongs to a different demographic — older, wealthier, more financial-savvy — and the exclusion feeling is preventing you from starting.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the belief that investing belongs to someone else. Not a lack of information — most people who delay investing know they should start. It's the felt sense that the room where those decisions are made is not one you're in.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I believe investing isn't really for someone like me, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the whole topic makes me feel like I'm on the outside of something, I am open to looking at this.
Even though I was never shown how to think about this, I am willing to start somewhere.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: Investing isn't something people like me do.
Side of Eye: It's complicated and I don't know enough.
Under Eye: I could lose everything if I get it wrong.
Under Nose: No one in my family did this.
Chin: I feel stupid even looking at it.
Collarbone: That world wasn't built for me.
Under Arm: It's easier to leave it in the current account.
Top of Head: I keep waiting until I feel ready.
Reframe
Eyebrow: Investing starts somewhere small — and I am in the right place.
Side of Eye: I can learn as I go — no one starts with full knowledge.
Under Eye: Starting with a small amount is not the same as putting everything at risk.
Under Nose: My family's pattern is not my ceiling.
Chin: I am allowed in this room.
Collarbone: The barriers are real and also not fixed.
Under Arm: Ready doesn't arrive before starting.
Top of Head: I can take one concrete step today.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
